meta.name: English Quest — Level Check bank
meta.note: Original items written for English Quest from public CEFR can-do descriptors. Not copied from any published test. Add items freely: each needs id, level, skill, q, o (4 options), a (index of correct). Reading items add p (passage). Listening items add audio (script the app speaks aloud).

questions.q: She ___ a student at a big school.
They ___ from Casablanca.
I get up ___ seven o'clock.
___ you like tea?
There ___ two books on the table.
This is my brother. ___ name is Karim.
We ___ television in the evening.
My father ___ in a bank.
I have got ___ orange in my bag.
___ is your birthday? — In May.
The cat is ___ the chair.
I ___ speak three languages.
Look! The children ___ in the garden.
She ___ got a red bicycle.
These are my ___.
He is a teacher. ___ works at my school.
We go to school ___ Monday.
I ___ hungry. Can I have a sandwich?
My mother's sister is my ___.
We eat breakfast in the ___.
The opposite of "hot" is ___.
You write with a ___.
Saturday and Sunday are the ___.
A baby dog is a ___.
I am thirsty. I want some ___.
The sky is ___ on a sunny day.
We cook food in the ___.
Twelve minus two is ___.
Someone says "Thank you very much." You say:
You meet your teacher at 9 a.m. You say:
You meet someone new. You say:
You want to know someone's name. You ask:
You leave your friend's house at night. You say:
Your friend says "How are you?" You say:

questions.audio: Hello! My name is Sara. I am fifteen years old and I live in Rabat.
questions.q: 🎧 How old is Sara?

questions.audio: I have two brothers and one sister. My sister's name is Nora.
questions.q: 🎧 How many brothers does the speaker have?

questions.audio: The English class starts at ten o'clock in room five.
questions.q: 🎧 Where is the English class?

questions.audio: I like tea, but I don't like coffee.
questions.q: 🎧 What does the speaker like?

questions.audio: It is raining today, so take your umbrella.
questions.q: 🎧 What is the weather like?

questions.audio: My favourite day is Friday because we play football after school.
questions.q: 🎧 What do they do on Friday?
Yesterday we ___ to the beach.
My brother is ___ than me.
I ___ visit my grandparents next weekend.
You ___ wear a helmet on a motorbike. It's the law.
She ___ like horror films.
This is the ___ restaurant in our town.
___ you ever ___ couscous? — Yes, many times!
We ___ TV when the lights went out.
There isn't ___ milk in the fridge.
I'm tired. I think I ___ go to bed early.
He plays tennis ___ than his sister.
You ___ smoke in the hospital. It's forbidden.
She was born ___ 2008.
How ___ sugar do you take in your tea?
I enjoy ___ to music while I study.
If you heat ice, it ___.
I bought bread at the ___.
The train was late, so we ___ the bus instead.
It rained all day, so the match was ___.
I need to ___ money from the bank machine.
She ___ her homework before dinner every day.
The film was so ___ that I fell asleep.
Can you turn ___ the lights? It's dark in here.
We stayed at a small ___ near the sea.
My phone is dead. I forgot to ___ it.
He was late because he ___ the bus.
Put on your ___ — it's cold outside.
The doctor gave me some ___ for my headache.
In a café, you want water. You say:
o: Could I have some water, please?

questions.q: Your friend looks lost. You say:
You want to ask for the price in a shop:
You didn't hear what someone said. You say:
You want to invite a friend to the cinema:
o: Would you like to see a film tonight?

questions.q: You bump into someone by accident. You say:

questions.audio: The museum opens at nine in the morning and closes at six in the evening. On Mondays it is closed all day.
questions.q: 🎧 When is the museum closed?

questions.audio: I wanted to buy the blue trainers, but they only had them in white, so I got the white ones for thirty euros.
questions.q: 🎧 Which trainers did the speaker buy?

questions.audio: Last summer we drove to the mountains instead of flying to the coast. It was cheaper and the views were amazing.
questions.q: 🎧 Why did they choose the mountains?

questions.audio: Your appointment is on Thursday at half past two. Please arrive ten minutes early.
questions.q: 🎧 What time is the appointment?

questions.audio: I used to walk to school, but now I cycle because it's faster and I can leave home later.
questions.q: 🎧 How does the speaker go to school now?

questions.audio: The concert was great, but we couldn't find parking, so next time we'll take the tram.
questions.q: 🎧 What was the problem?
I ___ my keys — I can't open the door!
If it rains tomorrow, we ___ the picnic.
She's lived here ___ 2019.
If I had more money, I ___ travel around Morocco.
I'm not used ___ up early on weekends.
The homework ___ before Friday.
He said he ___ tired and went home.
You've been to Marrakech, ___?
I stopped ___ coffee because I couldn't sleep.
This is the town ___ my father grew up.
While I ___ dinner, my phone rang three times.
She's not old ___ to drive yet.
I'd rather ___ at home tonight.
The meeting was ___ until next week because the manager was ill.
I came ___ an old photo of my grandfather while cleaning.
She takes ___ her mother — same smile, same laugh.
We need to ___ a decision before Friday.
The company is looking for someone ___ in social media.
Could you ___ me a favour and post this letter?
Petrol prices have ___ sharply this year.
I don't want to ___ your time — I'll be quick.
Your friend can't sleep before exams. You say:
o: If I were you, I'd stop drinking coffee at night.

questions.q: You want to politely refuse an invitation:
o: I'd love to, but I'm busy that day.

questions.q: Your order arrives cold at a restaurant. You say:
o: Excuse me, I'm afraid this is cold. Could you heat it up?

questions.q: A tourist asks about your city. You suggest:
o: You should definitely visit the old medina.

questions.q: You disagree with a classmate politely:
o: I see what you mean, but I look at it differently.

questions.p: Salma started a small online shop selling handmade soap. At first she had two orders a week. After a local influencer mentioned her page, she now struggles to keep up with demand and is looking for a helper.
questions.q: What changed Salma's business?

questions.p: Salma started a small online shop selling handmade soap. At first she had two orders a week. After a local influencer mentioned her page, she now struggles to keep up with demand and is looking for a helper.
questions.q: What is Salma's problem now?

questions.p: Salma started a small online shop selling handmade soap. At first she had two orders a week. After a local influencer mentioned her page, she now struggles to keep up with demand and is looking for a helper.
questions.q: What is Salma probably going to do next?

questions.p: Youssef's football club trains twice a week, but matches are always on Sunday mornings. His new part-time job at the bakery starts at 7 a.m. on weekends, so he has asked the coach to move him to the reserve team, which plays on Saturday afternoons.
questions.q: Why can't Youssef play on Sunday mornings?
o: He works at a bakery then

questions.p: Youssef's football club trains twice a week, but matches are always on Sunday mornings. His new part-time job at the bakery starts at 7 a.m. on weekends, so he has asked the coach to move him to the reserve team, which plays on Saturday afternoons.
questions.q: What solution did Youssef find?

questions.p: Youssef's football club trains twice a week, but matches are always on Sunday mornings. His new part-time job at the bakery starts at 7 a.m. on weekends, so he has asked the coach to move him to the reserve team, which plays on Saturday afternoons.
questions.q: When does the reserve team play?

questions.audio: I was going to order the fish, but the waiter recommended the lamb tagine, and honestly it was the best decision I've made all week.
questions.q: 🎧 What did the speaker eat?

questions.audio: The trip is on, but there's been a change: instead of leaving Friday evening, the coach now departs Saturday at dawn, so nobody misses Thursday's revision session.
questions.q: 🎧 Why was the departure changed?
o: So students can attend a revision session

questions.audio: I've been learning the guitar for six months. I still can't play fast songs, but slow ones sound quite good now, which keeps me motivated.
questions.q: 🎧 How does the speaker feel about their progress?

questions.audio: If the rain continues, tomorrow's ceremony will move to the sports hall, so please check the school website before you leave home.
questions.q: 🎧 What should people do before leaving home?
o: Go to the sports hall directly

questions.audio: My sister used to hate reading, but since she discovered detective novels she finishes two books a month and even joined a book club.
questions.q: 🎧 What changed the sister's attitude to reading?

questions.audio: The manager said the position requires weekend availability, which unfortunately rules me out because of my university classes on Saturdays.
questions.q: 🎧 Why can't the speaker take the job?
By the time we arrived, the tickets ___ out.
The new bridge ___ by the end of next year.
She asked me ___ the report.
If you ___ me earlier, I could have helped you.
I wish I ___ more attention in maths classes at school.
___ having no formal training, she paints remarkably well.
He must ___ the message — he replied within a minute.
It's the first time I ___ anything like this.
The suspect denied ___ anywhere near the bank that night.
___ he apologises, I won't speak to him again.
The documents, ___ were signed last week, are already out of date.
You ___ have booked in advance — the hall was half empty.
Rarely ___ such a talented group of students in one class.
The government plans to ___ stricter rules on plastic bags.
Negotiations broke ___ after neither side would compromise.
Her success is largely ___ to years of disciplined practice.
The evidence he presented was far from ___ — the jury remained unsure.
The two ministers held ___ talks that lasted late into the night.
Sales figures fell ___ short of the company's expectations.
She has a ___ knowledge of Moroccan history — ask her anything.
The committee ___ the proposal on the grounds of cost.
His constant interruptions ___ the flow of the meeting.
A colleague's idea won't work, and you want to disagree diplomatically:
o: I see your point, but I'm not sure it would work in practice.

questions.q: You need an extension on a deadline. The most appropriate request:
o: I was wondering whether it might be possible to have a few extra days.

questions.q: You want to interrupt a meeting politely:
o: Sorry to interrupt, but may I add something here?

questions.q: A friend's business plan seems risky. You warn them tactfully:
o: Have you considered what happens if sales are slower than expected?

questions.p: Remote work has blurred the line between office and home. Supporters praise the flexibility; critics note that many employees now answer messages late into the evening, effectively working longer for the same salary. A handful of firms have responded with 'right to disconnect' policies, though enforcement remains patchy.
questions.q: What do critics suggest about remote work?

questions.p: Remote work has blurred the line between office and home. Supporters praise the flexibility; critics note that many employees now answer messages late into the evening, effectively working longer for the same salary. A handful of firms have responded with 'right to disconnect' policies, though enforcement remains patchy.
questions.q: The phrase 'blurred the line' means the difference has become:

questions.p: Remote work has blurred the line between office and home. Supporters praise the flexibility; critics note that many employees now answer messages late into the evening, effectively working longer for the same salary. A handful of firms have responded with 'right to disconnect' policies, though enforcement remains patchy.
questions.q: What does 'enforcement remains patchy' imply about the policies?

questions.p: When the city introduced a bike-sharing scheme, sceptics predicted vandalism and empty docks. Two years on, ridership has tripled, and the loudest complaint is that there are not enough bikes at rush hour — a problem the operator, with some satisfaction, calls 'the right kind of failure'.
questions.q: What did sceptics originally expect?

questions.p: When the city introduced a bike-sharing scheme, sceptics predicted vandalism and empty docks. Two years on, ridership has tripled, and the loudest complaint is that there are not enough bikes at rush hour — a problem the operator, with some satisfaction, calls 'the right kind of failure'.
questions.q: Why does the operator feel 'satisfaction' about the complaint?
o: It proves the scheme is popular

questions.p: When the city introduced a bike-sharing scheme, sceptics predicted vandalism and empty docks. Two years on, ridership has tripled, and the loudest complaint is that there are not enough bikes at rush hour — a problem the operator, with some satisfaction, calls 'the right kind of failure'.
questions.q: 'The right kind of failure' refers to a problem caused by:

questions.audio: Look, I'm not saying the internship was a waste of time — the contacts alone were valuable — but had I known how little actual training was involved, I might have chosen the research post instead.
questions.q: 🎧 What is the speaker's overall view of the internship?

questions.audio: The flight itself was fine; it was the connection that ruined everything. Forty minutes was never going to be enough at an airport that size, and sure enough, we watched our plane leave from the wrong side of passport control.
questions.q: 🎧 What caused the problem?

questions.audio: Sales rose in March, dipped slightly through the summer, and only in October did we see the sharp recovery the board had been promised since spring.
questions.q: 🎧 When did the sharp recovery happen?

questions.audio: I'd hesitate to call the reviews unfair — the service genuinely was slow that week — but judging an entire restaurant on its worst three days strikes me as harsh.
questions.q: 🎧 The speaker thinks the reviews were:

questions.audio: The scholarship covers tuition and accommodation, but not travel, which, given that the university is on another continent, is a far bigger gap than the brochure makes it sound.
questions.q: 🎧 What is NOT covered by the scholarship?

questions.audio: Had the referee not intervened when he did, the argument between the two captains might well have emptied both benches.
questions.q: 🎧 What prevented the situation from escalating?
___ had the concert started when the power went out.
It's high time the city ___ its public transport.
Under no circumstances ___ passengers open the doors between stations.
Not only ___ the deadline, she also delivered under budget.
___ the committee to reject the plan, we would need an alternative within days.
He speaks as though he ___ there himself, though he wasn't.
The proposal, the details of ___ remain confidential, will be announced on Friday.
So absorbed ___ in the novel that she missed her station entirely.
I'd sooner you ___ mention this to anyone until it's confirmed.
___ as it may seem, the figures are accurate.
Only after the funds had cleared ___ the contracts released.
Much ___ I admire her ambition, the timeline strikes me as unrealistic.
The witness is believed ___ the country shortly after the incident.
The report ___ serious doubts on the project's feasibility.
Her argument, ___ persuasive, ignores the financial constraints.
The evidence for the new therapy remains ___ at best — larger trials are needed.
After the scandal, the minister had little choice but to ___ his position.
The lecture was so dense that even specialists struggled to ___ its central claim.
Critics accused the channel of ___ coverage that favoured one candidate.
Efforts to ___ the damage came too late to save the partnership.
The negotiations reached a ___ when neither side would discuss borders.
His ___ remarks at the funeral struck many as poorly judged.
You must decline a request from a senior colleague, in writing:
o: I'm afraid I won't be able to take this on at present, much as I'd like to help.

questions.q: You want to challenge a statistic in a formal debate without accusing anyone:
o: I'd want to see how that figure was arrived at before drawing conclusions.

questions.q: A client demands the impossible. You manage expectations:
o: What we can realistically commit to within that timeframe is the first phase.
You clearly don't understand our work.

questions.q: You concede a point in an argument while holding your position:
o: Granted, the costs are high — but the alternative is costlier still.

questions.p: That the committee reversed its decision surprised no one; what raised eyebrows was the speed with which it did so, barely a week after publicly defending the original policy. Insiders point to a single donor's intervention, though the official line speaks only of 'new information coming to light'.
questions.q: The writer implies the reversal was:
o: expected, but its timing was striking

questions.p: That the committee reversed its decision surprised no one; what raised eyebrows was the speed with which it did so, barely a week after publicly defending the original policy. Insiders point to a single donor's intervention, though the official line speaks only of 'new information coming to light'.
questions.q: What explanation do insiders give for the reversal?

questions.p: That the committee reversed its decision surprised no one; what raised eyebrows was the speed with which it did so, barely a week after publicly defending the original policy. Insiders point to a single donor's intervention, though the official line speaks only of 'new information coming to light'.
questions.q: The tone of the phrase 'the official line speaks only of' suggests the writer finds the official explanation:

questions.p: Language apps promise fluency in minutes a day, and millions oblige, streaks intact, vocabularies swelling. Yet ask these diligent streak-keepers to order a coffee abroad and many freeze — a reminder that recognising a word on a screen and retrieving it under social pressure are cousins, not twins.
questions.q: The writer's main point is that app-based learning:
o: builds recognition more than real-world fluency
is a complete waste of time

questions.p: Language apps promise fluency in minutes a day, and millions oblige, streaks intact, vocabularies swelling. Yet ask these diligent streak-keepers to order a coffee abroad and many freeze — a reminder that recognising a word on a screen and retrieving it under social pressure are cousins, not twins.
questions.q: The metaphor 'cousins, not twins' means the two skills are:

questions.p: Language apps promise fluency in minutes a day, and millions oblige, streaks intact, vocabularies swelling. Yet ask these diligent streak-keepers to order a coffee abroad and many freeze — a reminder that recognising a word on a screen and retrieving it under social pressure are cousins, not twins.
questions.q: 'Millions oblige' most nearly means millions:

questions.audio: I won't pretend the merger came out of nowhere — the rumours had been circulating for months — but the terms, frankly, were harsher than even the pessimists among us had predicted.
questions.q: 🎧 What surprised the speaker about the merger?

questions.audio: Her thesis, for all its stylistic elegance, rests on a dataset so narrow that one hesitates to generalise beyond the three schools she studied.
questions.q: 🎧 The speaker's main criticism concerns the thesis's:

questions.audio: Suffice it to say that when the auditors left, three department heads updated their CVs — and by Friday, two of them no longer needed to.
questions.q: 🎧 What most likely happened by Friday?
o: Two department heads lost their jobs

questions.audio: Whatever you make of his politics — and I make rather little of them — his command of a hostile room is something colleagues twenty years his senior still can't match.
questions.q: 🎧 The speaker admires the man's:

questions.audio: The restoration divided the town: purists mourned the original stonework, while pragmatists pointed out that without the intervention there would soon have been nothing left to mourn.
questions.q: 🎧 What was the pragmatists' argument?
o: The building would have been lost without restoration

questions.audio: Far be it from me to question the panel's expertise, but awarding first prize to a project that never actually ran does invite a certain scepticism.
questions.q: 🎧 The speaker's tone towards the panel's decision is:
___ it not been for the goalkeeper, they would have lost by five.
___ was her confidence that no one dared question the figures.
It was only when the funding dried up ___ the project's flaws became undeniable.
Try ___ he might, he could not reconcile the two accounts.
Little ___ that the letter she almost discarded would change her career.
He insisted that the report ___ verbatim, errors and all.
___ the negotiations collapse entirely, contingency funding is in place.
Nowhere in the contract ___ any mention of overtime.
Scarcely ___ the ink dried on the agreement than both sides accused each other of breaching it.
What the reviewers objected to was not the method but ___ it had been applied.
So intricate ___ the fraud that even forensic accountants took months to unpick it.
He would sooner the whole archive ___ than see it sold piecemeal.
___ as the losses were, the reputational damage proved costlier still.
His apology struck many as ___ — polished, but with no real regret behind it.
The novel's charm lies in its ___ prose — plain on the surface, yet quietly devastating.
The two witnesses gave accounts that ___ in every important detail.
Diplomats prized her ability to deliver hard messages with ___.
The regime's ___ concessions fooled no one; repression continued unabated.
Years of neglect had left the manuscript in a ___ state, its pages crumbling at a touch.
Her ___ for understatement made her rare praise all the more valuable.
The committee's report was a masterpiece of ___ — three hundred pages that committed to nothing.
He treated the rules with ___ disregard, as if consequences were for other people.
You suspect a proposal is dishonest but cannot prove it. The most defensible phrasing:
o: Certain aspects of the proposal would benefit from closer scrutiny.
I refuse to discuss this nonsense.

questions.q: You must correct your own published error with maximum credibility:
o: An earlier version overstated the figures; the corrected data appear below.
The error was too small to matter.

questions.q: A journalist asks a question you cannot legally answer. You respond:
o: You'll appreciate that ongoing proceedings prevent me from commenting on that.

questions.q: You praise a rival's work while subtly noting its limits:
o: An impressive study — within the boundaries it sets itself.
Better than I expected from them.
Everyone says it's good so it must be.

questions.p: The museum's new wing has been hailed as bold. Bold it certainly is — in the way a demolition is bold. One leaves admiring the architect's nerve, if not his judgement, and wondering whether the committee that approved the design ever stood where visitors now must: squinting into glare, hunting for an entrance the building seems determined to conceal.
questions.q: The reviewer's overall tone is:

questions.p: The museum's new wing has been hailed as bold. Bold it certainly is — in the way a demolition is bold. One leaves admiring the architect's nerve, if not his judgement, and wondering whether the committee that approved the design ever stood where visitors now must: squinting into glare, hunting for an entrance the building seems determined to conceal.
questions.q: 'His nerve, if not his judgement' suggests the architect is:

questions.p: The museum's new wing has been hailed as bold. Bold it certainly is — in the way a demolition is bold. One leaves admiring the architect's nerve, if not his judgement, and wondering whether the committee that approved the design ever stood where visitors now must: squinting into glare, hunting for an entrance the building seems determined to conceal.
questions.q: The final image of visitors 'hunting for an entrance' chiefly serves to:
o: suggest the design ignores practical experience

questions.p: It has become fashionable to lament the death of the essay, usually in essays of considerable length. The form, we are told, cannot survive the scroll; attention, like topsoil, has eroded. Yet the eulogies keep finding readers, which rather suggests the patient is not so much dying as changing clothes.
questions.q: The writer finds the 'death of the essay' claims:

questions.p: It has become fashionable to lament the death of the essay, usually in essays of considerable length. The form, we are told, cannot survive the scroll; attention, like topsoil, has eroded. Yet the eulogies keep finding readers, which rather suggests the patient is not so much dying as changing clothes.
questions.q: The metaphor 'changing clothes' implies the essay is:

questions.p: It has become fashionable to lament the death of the essay, usually in essays of considerable length. The form, we are told, cannot survive the scroll; attention, like topsoil, has eroded. Yet the eulogies keep finding readers, which rather suggests the patient is not so much dying as changing clothes.
questions.q: The irony noted in the first sentence is that the lament:
o: takes the very form it declares dead

questions.audio: To describe the reorganisation as a success requires a definition of success flexible enough to include losing a third of the engineering staff — which, to be fair, the press release very nearly manages.
questions.q: 🎧 The speaker's attitude to the press release is:

questions.audio: She has that rarest of judicial gifts: the ability to make the losing party feel heard, at precisely the moment she dismantles their argument beyond repair.
questions.q: 🎧 What paradoxical skill does the judge have?
o: Making losers feel respected while defeating them

questions.audio: Whether the manuscript is genuine is almost beside the point; its influence on three generations of scholars who believed it was is a historical fact no authentication can undo.
questions.q: 🎧 The speaker argues the manuscript's importance rests on:

questions.audio: The minister's denial was categorical, which in this administration usually indicates the story is not merely true, but worse than reported.
questions.q: 🎧 The speaker treats categorical denials as:

questions.audio: His lectures were exercises in strategic digression: by the time he wandered back to the syllabus, you'd learned three things more valuable than anything on it.
questions.q: 🎧 The speaker views the digressions as:

questions.audio: One does not so much read her footnotes as descend into them, emerging hours later, blinking, having forgotten the argument but somehow trusting it more.
questions.q: 🎧 The description of the footnotes is best characterised as:
